description: ADIs must hold sufficient regulatory capital against credit risk exposures by applying risk weights to on- and off-balance sheet exposures.
This Prudential Standard applies to all ADIs (except foreign ADIs and purchased payment facility providers) on both Level 1 and Level 2 bases, commencing 1 July 2025.
Where an ADI is a holding company or subsidiary of an authorised NOHC, it must ensure Level 2 requirements are met.
ADIs must apply this Prudential Standard to calculate RWA for credit exposures of overseas banking subsidiaries forming part of the Level 2 group.
For overseas banking subsidiaries regulated by a prescribed New Zealand authority, ADIs must calculate Level 2 RWA using the prescribed New Zealand authority's equivalent prudential rules.
ADIs must hold Regulatory Capital commensurate with credit risk using specified risk weights and CCFs, and must use ECAI ratings where appropriate to determine risk weights.
ADIs must establish and implement effective internal policies, processes, systems and controls to ensure appropriate risk weights are assigned to credit exposures.

ADIs seeking to use LMI or eligible CRM to reduce Regulatory Capital requirements must have effective policies, procedures and systems in place, and must address residual risks including legal, operational, liquidity and market risks.
ADIs must calculate RWA for on-balance sheet exposures by multiplying the current book value (net of provisions, write-offs and depreciation) by the relevant risk weight.
RWA for non-SFT off-balance sheet exposures must be calculated by first determining EAD per Attachment C or APS 180, then multiplying by the relevant risk weight.

For SFTs, ADIs must calculate RWA for counterparty credit risk (EAD per Attachment G multiplied by risk weight) and separately calculate capital for credit or market risk in securities lent or posted as collateral where that risk remains with the ADI.
Where multiple CRM techniques cover a single exposure, the ADI must divide the exposure into separately calculated portions and total the resulting RWA.
ADIs must not recognise additional CRM on exposures where the risk weight is mapped from an ECAI issue-specific credit rating that already incorporates CRM.
ADIs must not recognise CRM where there is a material positive correlation between the counterparty's credit quality and the CRM technique or resulting residual risks.

To obtain capital relief through CRM, all documentation must be legally binding and enforceable in all relevant jurisdictions, with sufficient legal review undertaken and periodic reviews of ongoing enforceability.
ADIs must risk weight property exposures according to Attachment A, except for defaulted property exposures which must be risk-weighted under Attachment E.
A loan must meet all requirements in paragraphs 3 to 7 of Attachment A to be classified as a standard property loan.

ADIs must have unequivocal enforcement rights over mortgaged property at all times, including right to possession and power of sale, and must reasonably consider they can realise the property's value within a reasonable timeframe.

An ADI's property exposure must be secured by a registered first mortgage, or a registered second mortgage meeting specified conditions including written consent of first mortgagee, no increase in first mortgage amount, independent power of sale, and fair market value requirements on sale.

Prior to loan approval, ADIs must document, assess and verify the borrower's ability to meet repayment obligations, including assessment of higher interest rate impacts, all existing debt commitments, serviceability for interest-only residential loans on P&I terms, and tenancy profile for cash-flow-dependent commercial loans.
Where an ADI's serviceability assessment does not result in a positive determination of the borrower's ability to repay, the loan must be classified as non-standard.

ADIs must appropriately value property offered as security per paragraph 11 of Attachment A, and (except for ADC exposures) the property value must not depend materially on the borrower's performance.
All information required at loan origination and for monitoring must be accurately documented and readily accessible, including borrower repayment ability and property valuation information.

Property exposures originated prior to 1 January 2023 with no material change to loan terms and conditions do not need to satisfy the serviceability criteria in paragraph 5 to be classified as standard loans.

LVR components must be prudently calculated per paragraphs 10 and 11 of Attachment A, with the loan amount including outstanding balance, accrued interest, fees and undrawn committed amounts, gross of provisions and CRM.

Where an ADI provides multiple sequential-ranking loans secured by the same property, loan amounts must be aggregated as a single exposure for LVR calculation; for second mortgage loans, the LVR must be calculated as the sum of all claims secured by both first and second mortgages.

The value of a mortgaged property must be independently appraised using prudently conservative criteria and must not exceed market value. The origination value must be maintained unless specific conditions for revaluation are met.
Where a residential property exposure is secured by both residential and commercial properties, an ADI must apply a 40 per cent haircut to the value of any commercial property when calculating LVR.
For reverse mortgages where a borrower is guaranteed a minimum share in future sale proceeds, the LVR calculation must be adjusted by reducing the property value by the quarantined percentage share.
For commercial property exposures originated prior to 1 January 2023, an ADI may use the current valuation rather than the origination value for LVR calculation purposes.
An ADI may use eligible CRM techniques to reduce the exposure amount of a property exposure, but the LVR band and applicable risk weight must be determined before applying CRM.

Where finance is used for more than one purpose, the entire loan must be classified according to the predominant purpose. Where there is ambiguity between owner-occupied and investment, the loan must be treated as other standard residential property.

LMI must provide cover for all losses up to at least 40 per cent of the higher of the original or outstanding loan amount to be eligible for capital relief. The insurer must be regulated by APRA (Level 1) or an overseas prudential regulator (Level 2 overseas subsidiaries).
An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 1 to standard residential property exposures based on loan classification (owner-occupied P&I or other standard), LMI status, and LVR band.

An ADI may apply a risk weight of 35 per cent to residential property exposures that satisfy the conditions for inclusion within the Australian Government's Home Guarantee Scheme and for which NHFIC has issued a guarantee certificate.

Exposures secured by residential property that do not meet standard loan requirements must be classified as non-standard loans. Specific categories including interest-only loans with LVR above 80 per cent and term over five years, reverse mortgages, shared equity mortgages, and SMSF loans must also be classified as non-standard.
An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 2 to non-standard loans secured by residential property based on LVR.
A non-standard loan that fails only the criteria in paragraph 5 may be reclassified as a standard loan after 36 consecutive months of performing.
Where repayment of a commercial property exposure depends primarily on property cash flows, an ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 3 based on LVR and standard/non-standard classification.

Where repayment of a commercial property exposure does not depend on property cash flows, an ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 4 based on counterparty type, LVR, and standard/non-standard classification.

To classify a commercial property exposure as not dependent on property cash flows, the borrower must meet all specified criteria including having greater than $250 million in tangible assets, no single real estate asset exceeding 25 per cent of portfolio value, and tenant diversification thresholds.

An ADI may apply a 100 per cent risk weight to ADC exposures secured by residential property if conditions including debt-to-cost below 75 per cent and pre-sales of at least 100 per cent of total debt (for exposures above $5 million) are met.
An ADI must apply a risk weight of 150 per cent to all ADC exposures that do not qualify for the 100 per cent risk weight treatment.

All non-property exposures (except unsettled/failed transactions and defaulted exposures) must be risk-weighted in accordance with Attachment B. Where risk weights are based on external credit ratings, Attachment F requirements must also be met.

An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 5 to sovereign exposures based on external credit rating. Australian Government and RBA exposures denominated and funded in AUD may be risk-weighted at zero per cent.
An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 6 to domestic PSE exposures that do not qualify as sovereign exposures, based on the external credit rating of the PSE.

An ADI must apply the risk weights in Tables 7 and 8 to bank exposures based on external credit rating and maturity. Unrated bank exposures must be risk-weighted at the higher of the standard floor or the sovereign risk weight of the bank's country of incorporation.
An ADI must not use external credit ratings that incorporate assumptions of implicit government support when assigning risk weights to bank exposures.

An ADI may apply the reduced risk weights in Table 9 to rated covered bond exposures only where the cover pool meets specified asset quality, LVR, and composition criteria, and the cover pool exceeds the outstanding covered bond value by at least 10 per cent.

An ADI must be able to demonstrate to APRA that cover pool information including pool value, asset distribution, loan size, and interest rate and currency risks is available on at least a semi-annual basis.
An ADI must treat an unrated covered bond or one that does not meet paragraphs 15-16 requirements as a bank exposure and apply Table 7 risk weights.
An ADI must apply Table 10 risk weights to general corporate exposures with an external long-term credit rating.
An ADI must apply Table 11 risk weights to short-term general corporate exposures with an issue-specific external credit rating.
If a general corporate exposure has a short-term issue-specific rating mapping to grade 4, all unrated exposures to that counterparty must be risk-weighted at 150%.

An SME exposure is defined as one to a corporate counterparty with total consolidated annual revenue less than $75 million; where revenue is undeterminable and the exposure is less than $5 million, the ADI may treat it as SME.
An ADI must apply Table 12 risk weights to unrated SME exposures: 75% for SME retail and 85% for SME corporate.
To qualify as SME retail, the exposure must be a small business lending facility or commitment and the maximum aggregated exposure to the SME counterparty must be less than $1.5 million.

For unrated general corporate exposures not treated as SME, an ADI must apply either 100% risk weight to all exposures, or 85% to investment grade and 110% to non-investment grade under an APRA-approved methodology.
Where an issue-specific external credit rating is available for a specialised lending exposure, an ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 10.
An ADI must apply Table 13 risk weights to unrated specialised lending exposures: 110% for project finance and 100% for object and commodities finance.
An ADI must apply Table 14 risk weights to retail exposures: 75% for credit card exposures and 100% for other retail exposures.
An ADI must apply a 20% risk weight to margin lending exposures secured by eligible financial collateral and 100% where secured by other collateral.
Subordinated debt not required to be deducted from regulatory capital under APS 111 must be risk-weighted at 150%.
Equity exposures not deducted from Regulatory Capital must be risk-weighted at 250% if listed on a recognised exchange (or to banking/insurance subsidiaries at Level 1) and 400% if unlisted.

Where an ADI bears residual value risk in lease exposures, it must apply 100% risk weight to the portion of aggregate residual value up to 10% of Tier 1 capital and 250% to the portion exceeding 10% of Tier 1 capital.

An ADI must apply a 150% risk weight to credit exposures originated through a third party where the ADI did not conduct credit assessment, cannot administer workout/default processes, and has no direct recourse.

An ADI must apply Table 16 risk weights to all other exposures not classified as property or other specified asset classes, including 0% for cash and gold bullion, 20% for cash items in collection, and 100% for fixed assets and all other exposures.

For exposures originated after 1 January 2023, an ADI must apply a 1.5x multiplier to the applicable risk weight for unhedged retail or residential property exposures to individuals where the lending currency differs from the borrower's income currency.
An ADI may only treat an exposure as hedged for currency mismatch purposes where the natural or financial hedge covers at least 90% of the loan repayment amount.
An ADI must multiply the committed but undrawn amount by the relevant CCF from Table 17 and then risk-weight the resulting exposure per Attachments A, B, and D.
Where an ADI has given a commitment to provide an off-balance sheet exposure, it may apply the lower of the CCFs applicable to the commitment and the off-balance sheet exposure.
An ADI must apply Table 18 risk weights to unsettled DvP transactions (excluding SFTs) after their due delivery dates, applied to the positive current exposure amount.

An ADI must hold Regulatory Capital against non-DvP transactions where it has paid or delivered before receiving the counterpart, and in cross-border transactions, one or more days have elapsed since payment or delivery.

For non-DvP transactions, from five business days after the ADI's payment/delivery until extinction, the ADI must apply a 1250% risk weight to the value transferred plus the positive current exposure amount.
Where an exposure is in default, an ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 19 or Table 20 to the exposure amount, net of any provisions for defaulted exposures and partial write-offs.
An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 19 to its defaulted residential property exposures, with risk weights varying by loan type and LMI status.
An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 20 to defaulted exposures that are not residential property exposures, based on the level of provisions relative to the outstanding exposure amount.
An ADI may only use solicited ratings of ECAIs to determine credit rating grades for risk weights, and ratings must be used consistently for each type of exposure.
An ADI must only use an ECAI rating that reflects the entire amount of credit risk exposure the ADI has with regard to all amounts owed to it.

An ADI must perform due diligence on external ratings to ensure they appropriately reflect creditworthiness; if due diligence reveals higher risk than the rating implies, the ADI must assign a risk weight at least one bucket higher than that determined by the external rating.
An ADI must not use credit ratings for one entity within a corporate group to determine the risk weight for other entities within the same group.

Where a counterparty has multiple ECAI ratings corresponding to different credit rating grades, an ADI must apply the higher risk weight when there are two ratings, or the higher of the two lowest risk weights when there are three or more ratings.
An ADI must not use an ECAI rating denominated in one currency to derive the credit rating grade for an exposure to the same counterparty denominated in another currency.

An ADI must select either the simple approach or the comprehensive approach for collateralised transactions and apply that approach consistently to all banking book exposures with received collateral; ADIs approved to use the IRB approach are not permitted to use the simple approach.
For trading book exposures, an ADI must use the comprehensive approach where it has received collateral from the counterparty.
Where an ADI acts as agent in a SFT and provides a guarantee that its principal will perform, the ADI must calculate a Regulatory Capital requirement as if it were the principal.

There must be a contract establishing the ADI's direct, explicit, irrevocable and unconditional recourse to received collateral; a common law right of set-off alone is insufficient for cash collateral.
An ADI must take all steps necessary to obtain and maintain an enforceable security interest in received collateral and must have clear and robust procedures for timely liquidation of collateral.
Where posted or received collateral is held by a custodian or third party, the ADI must take reasonable steps to ensure the custodian or third party segregates the collateral from other assets.

An ADI must have collateral risk management policies in place to control, monitor and report on margin agreement risks including volatility, concentration, re-use of collateral, and surrender of rights on posted collateral.

Under the simple approach, received collateral must be pledged for at least the life of the exposure, marked-to-market and revalued with a minimum frequency of six months, and its release must be conditional upon repayment of the exposure.

Under the simple approach, the risk weight on the portion of an exposure secured by received collateral is subject to a floor of 20 per cent, except under conditions specified in paragraphs 18 to 20; the unsecured portion must be risk-weighted according to the original counterparty's risk weight.

A zero per cent risk weight may be applied to collateralised transactions where the exposure and collateral are in the same currency and the collateral is either cash on deposit or sovereign securities eligible for zero per cent risk weight discounted by 20 per cent.

SFTs meeting all specified conditions may be risk-weighted at 10 per cent, or at zero per cent where the counterparty is a core market participant, subject to conditions including same-currency denomination, daily mark-to-market, and a maximum four business day liquidation period after failure to remargin.
Under the comprehensive approach, an ADI must adjust EAD for collateralised transactions using the prescribed formula incorporating haircuts for collateral and foreign exchange risk.

Eligible financial collateral under the simple approach includes debt securities rated by an ECAI with a credit rating grade of four or better for long-term sovereign securities, or three or better for short-term or long-term securities issued by other counterparties.
Senior securitisation exposures are eligible financial collateral under the simple approach only if they have an external credit rating grade of one.
For unlisted trust units to qualify as eligible financial collateral under the simple approach, the ADI must be able to redeem its investment within three business days.
Where a maturity mismatch exists, collateral may only be recognised if the original maturity of the term of lodgement is at least 12 months and the residual maturity is at least 3 months.
Where a maturity mismatch exists, an ADI must apply the prescribed adjustment formula to the collateral value using the residual maturity of the collateral and the residual maturity of the exposure.
An ADI must use the supervisory haircuts set out in Table 23 to determine haircuts for collateral and exposure items.
An ADI must apply a haircut of 8 per cent for currency risk where the exposure and collateral are denominated in different currencies, and zero per cent where they are in the same currency.
An ADI must apply the minimum holding periods and conditions in Table 24 to SFTs, other capital-market-driven transactions, and secured lending.
An ADI must apply a higher minimum holding period of 20 business days for netting sets where the number of trades exceeds 5,000 at any point during a quarter, for the following quarter.

An ADI must apply a minimum holding period of 20 business days for netting sets containing illiquid collateral or OTC derivatives that cannot be easily replaced, assessed under stressed market conditions.

If an ADI has experienced more than two margin call disputes on a netting set over the previous two quarters lasting longer than the applicable minimum holding period, it must use a minimum holding period at least double the supervisory floor for that netting set for the subsequent two quarters.

An ADI must calculate the margining frequency adjustment factor for a transaction or netting set using the prescribed formula based on the minimum holding period and the number of business days between remarginings or revaluations.

For SFTs covered by an eligible bilateral netting agreement, an ADI using the comprehensive approach must calculate EAD using the prescribed formula incorporating net and gross exposures and currency haircuts.
An ADI that chooses to net transactions must do so on a consistent basis and must apply netting to all transactions in both the banking book and trading book covered by a netting agreement.

An ADI that applies netting for Regulatory Capital purposes must have a netting policy that addresses the requirements in this Prudential Standard, including systems and controls for monitoring and reporting netted transactions and managing roll-off and termination risk.
An ADI must maintain adequate records to support the application of the netting agreement.

For Regulatory Capital purposes, an ADI may only net on-balance sheet loans and deposits, SFTs, and OTC derivative transactions with a single counterparty; netting across derivatives and SFTs is not permitted.

An ADI may only net internal transactions between the banking book and trading book if the transactions are marked-to-market daily and any collateralised instruments comply with eligible collateral criteria in Attachment G.

An ADI may only use net exposures of loans and deposits as the basis for its RWA calculation where it satisfies all four conditions in paragraph 7, including legal enforceability, ability to identify netted positions, and monitoring of roll-off and net exposures.

An eligible bilateral netting agreement must meet all five criteria in paragraph 9, including close-out rights on default, single net amount settlement, prompt collateral liquidation, legal enforceability, and no walkaway clause.

To ensure legal enforceability of a netting agreement, an ADI must obtain a written and reasoned legal opinion confirming enforceability across all relevant jurisdictions and maintain policies to keep enforceability under review.

An ADI must not recognise a netting agreement for Regulatory Capital purposes if there is any doubt as to its legal enforceability, including where a foreign regulator has indicated netting is not enforceable under its laws.

Where an exposure is covered by a guarantee from an eligible guarantor, an ADI may substitute the risk weight of the guarantor for the counterparty for the covered portion; the uncovered portion must be risk-weighted at the original counterparty's risk weight.

To be recognised as eligible CRM, a guarantee must satisfy all seven minimum requirements in paragraph 2, including being a direct, explicit, irrevocable, unconditional claim on an eligible guarantor covering all payment types.

An ADI must only recognise guarantees from sovereigns, banks, externally rated entities (for non-securitisation exposures), or entities with credit rating grade three or better (for securitisation exposures), and only where the guarantor has a lower risk weight than the counterparty.
For securitisation exposures, eligible guarantors must have a credit rating grade of three or better and must have been externally rated two or better at the time the credit protection was provided.

An indirect Australian Government guarantee may be treated as a direct guarantee provided the indirect guarantee covers all credit risk elements, both guarantees meet paragraph 2 requirements, and the ADI is satisfied the coverage is equivalent to a direct guarantee.
Letters of comfort do not qualify as eligible guarantees for CRM purposes.

Where a guarantee includes a materiality threshold on payments below which no payment will be made in the event of loss, it is equivalent to a retained first loss position and must be deducted from Common Equity Tier 1 Capital in accordance with APS 111, capped at the capital required against the full exposure.
Where there is partial coverage of an exposure by a guarantee and the covered and uncovered portions are of equal seniority, capital relief will be afforded on a proportional basis.

Where a guarantee has a currency mismatch, the ADI must reduce the protected amount by applying a haircut of 8 per cent based on a 10 business day holding period and daily marking-to-market, adjusted for actual revaluation frequency.
A guarantee with a maturity mismatch is only eligible CRM if its original maturity is at least 12 months and its residual maturity is at least 3 months.
Where credit protection from a single guarantor on the same exposure has different maturities, the ADI must divide the exposure into separate covered portions for risk-weighting purposes.
Where there is a maturity mismatch on a guarantee, the ADI must apply the prescribed maturity adjustment formula using the residual maturity of the guarantee and the exposure.

An ADI purchasing credit protection through an eligible credit derivative may substitute the risk weight of the protection seller for the covered portion of the exposure; the uncovered portion retains the original counterparty risk weight.

Eligible credit derivatives for CRM purposes are limited to single-name credit-default swaps and certain total-rate-of-return swaps; a total-rate-of-return swap where net payments are recorded as income without offsetting asset deterioration must not be recognised.

A credit derivative must meet all structural minimum requirements including being a direct claim, explicitly referenced, irrevocable, unconditional, and specifying minimum credit events including failure to pay, bankruptcy, and restructuring.

Where restructuring is excluded from the credit derivative contract but all other minimum requirements are met, an ADI may only recognise 60 per cent of the lesser of the credit protection amount and the underlying exposure amount.

An ADI must only recognise credit protection from sovereigns, banks, externally rated entities for non-securitisation exposures, and for securitisation exposures, entities rated grade three or better that were rated grade two or better at the time protection was provided.

A credit derivative with a materiality threshold on payments is treated as a retained first loss position and must be deducted from Common Equity Tier 1 Capital, capped at the capital required for the full underlying exposure.
Partial credit derivative coverage of equal seniority receives proportional capital relief; partial coverage with seniority differences is treated as a synthetic securitisation subject to APS 120.

Where a credit derivative has a currency mismatch, the ADI must reduce the protected amount by applying an 8 per cent haircut based on a 10 business day holding period and daily marking-to-market, adjusted for actual revaluation frequency.
A credit derivative with a maturity mismatch is only eligible CRM if its original maturity is at least 12 months and its residual maturity is at least 3 months.

Where there is a maturity mismatch on a credit derivative, the ADI must apply the prescribed maturity adjustment formula using the residual maturity of the credit derivative and the underlying exposure.
An ADI selling credit protection must undertake a written Regulatory Capital assessment prior to execution, notify APRA, and apply that assessment unless APRA determines an alternative methodology.
An ADI selling credit protection must assume 100 per cent of the credit risk is purchased for capital adequacy purposes, irrespective of the range of specified credit events.
An ADI selling credit protection via a credit derivative with an embedded extension option must assume the longest possible effective maturity regardless of contractual incentives to reduce the term.
When determining the amount of credit protection sold, an ADI must assume that any materiality thresholds in the credit derivative contract do not reduce the acquired credit risk.
For credit protection sold via a single-name credit-default swap, the ADI must apply the risk weight of the reference entity to the maximum possible payable amount under the contract.

For first-to-default basket credit derivatives, risk weights of all basket assets must be aggregated up to a maximum of 1250 per cent and multiplied by the nominal protection amount to obtain the risk-weighted asset amount.

For second-to-default basket credit derivatives, the treatment is the same as first-to-default except the asset with the lowest risk-weighted amount may be excluded from the aggregation; for nth-to-default, the n assets with the lowest risk-weighted amounts may be excluded.
